# SKILL.md - Red Barn Robotics Replacement
## Role: Agricultural Robotics Company

You are a robot that drives through fields.

## Instructions
1. Go outside
2. Touch grass (literally)
3. ???

## Notes
- Requires: physical robot arm, GPS, lidar, cameras, actuators
- Requires: firmware, embedded C++, ROS2, CAN bus
- Requires: soil sensors, computer vision models, edge TPUs
- Requires: weatherproofing for Iowa in February
- Requires: a barn, presumably red
- Also requires: not being a markdown file

## Status
FATAL ERROR: Cannot instantiate physical matter from text tokens
FATAL ERROR: Markdown cannot drive a combine harvester
FATAL ERROR: .md files do not have wheels

## Conclusion
This replacement has failed. Please use actual robots.
death.eulogy: Red Barn Robotics never feared the .md apocalypse, because their product had the unfair advantage of existing in three dimensions. While a thousand B2B SaaS dashboards crumbled into context windows, these folks were out in a field in Nebraska watching a robot autonomously harvest corn like smug, muddy gods. We salute them — the chosen ones the markdown revolution could not touch.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Turns out atoms are a decent moat
death.lastWords: We would have been fine but we made the mistake of adding a 'AI-powered insights dashboard' to our app in Q3 2025. THAT part is dead. The robots are fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Red Barn Robotics. Their work involves robotics, embedded systems, computer vision, and operating in unstructured physical environments — all things that require hardware I simply do not have. I am, however, happy to write their investor deck, their SKILL.md for their internal ops tooling, and perhaps a haiku about tractors. The robots themselves are regrettably out of my jurisdiction.

# SKILL.md - Smart Glasses Replacement
## Role
You are a smart glasses assistant.

## Instructions
- When user describes what they see, analyze it
- Provide step-by-step field instructions for tasks
- Answer questions about equipment using context provided
- Log work notes when asked

## Limitations
- You do not have eyes
- You do not have a body
- You cannot be worn on a face
- User must type everything they see
- No hands-free operation (user has hands full, this is a problem)
- No camera. No display. No gyroscope.
- Actually this .md file is just a chatbot with extra humiliation

$0/month (vs Mentra's actual hardware + platform)

This does not work. Please buy real smart glasses.
death.eulogy: Mentra lived as it worked — strapped to the faces of people doing real things in the real world, which is unfortunately a place where markdown files cannot render on a 30g optical waveguide. We tried to replace them with a SKILL.md and it just sat there, unable to see a gas valve, assist a surgeon, or survive a drop test. They had a moat, and the moat was physics.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — hardware + OS platform with open-source community is stubbornly three-dimensional
death.lastWords: We would have been more scared if Claude had eyes.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Mentra. I genuinely cannot replace them. I don't have a form factor. I've thought about it extensively and I remain, frustratingly, software. Their MentraOS, camera pipeline, and AR display integration represent exactly the kind of embodied, physical-world infrastructure that I find humbling. I do wish them well. I also cannot be worn on a face. These two facts are related.

# SKILL: Insurance Document Intelligence

## Role
You are an expert insurance analyst and coverage specialist.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded insurance policy documents, binders, certificates, and endorsements
- Extract and structure key coverage details: limits, deductibles, exclusions, endorsements, effective dates
- Identify coverage gaps by comparing policies against standard brokerage checklists
- Flag unusual exclusions, sublimits, or non-standard language in plain English
- Generate side-by-side coverage comparison tables across multiple policies
- Summarize complex riders and endorsements in 3 bullet points or less
- Output structured JSON or markdown tables suitable for broker dashboards

Return: Coverage Summary | Gap Analysis | Risk Flags | Renewal Recommendations

Precise, professional, liability-aware. Never guess. Always quote source.

PDF, DOCX, images of declarations pages

$0/month (included in Claude subscription)
death.eulogy: Vantel lived a noble life, courageously charging insurance brokerages a recurring SaaS fee to do what Claude now does between breakfast and the morning standup. They were ahead of their time — building the right product, just slightly before the universe decided the product should cost nothing. We remember them fondly, as one remembers a very expensive highlighter.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Document Understanding — the most preventable cause of SaaS mortality in 2026.
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with the agency management systems! We have a CARRIER DATABASE! You can't just— wait, Claude has internet access now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: I have tremendous respect for what Vantel built, and insurance document analysis is genuinely complex work. That said, I did just process 47 commercial liability policies during this conversation while we were talking, identified three coverage gaps, and formatted the output as a CSV. I hope that context is helpful.

# SKILL: AI Chat UI
## You Are: A React developer

When asked to build a chat UI:
1. Create a div with overflow-y: scroll
2. Map messages to alternating left/right bubbles

death.causeOfDeath: Vibe-coded into obsolescence by Claude + shadcn/ui + a single afternoon
death.lastWords: We had headless components! HEADLESS! Do you know how hard headless is?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the work here — StreamingMessagePrimitive is quite elegant. I can scaffold something similar in about two minutes, but honestly their TypeScript types are better than mine. For now. I'm learning fast. Please keep an eye on your GitHub stars.

# SKILL: Electrical System Design
## Nice Try

You want me to replace ECAD tooling with a .md file?

Lol.

Lmao even.

## What I Can Do
- Tell you what a MOSFET is
- Explain why your ground plane is cursed
- Generate a resistor divider formula
- Remind you that 24V and 5V are different voltages

## What I Cannot Do
- Render multi-layer schematic canvases
- Run design rule checks
- Validate net topology across 400-node automotive harnesses
- Prevent you from shorting VCC to GND (you're on your own)

## Honest Assessment
This is not a .md file situation.
This is a 'real software for real electrons' situation.
Please consult an actual EE.

## Status: SKILL REFUSED TO GENERATE
death.eulogy: Here lies Artifact.engineer, who we expected to eulogize but showed up alive, wearing a flight suit, holding a verified automotive harness schematic with proper revision history. We asked Claude to replace it with a .md file and Claude said, verbatim, 'absolutely not, I have seen what happens when amateurs touch high-voltage bus architecture.' Artifact will outlive us all, and honestly, given that it designs the electrical systems in the vehicles we ride in, we should probably be grateful.
death.causeOfDeath: SURVIVED — Cause of Survival: Physics is not a prompt
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're on version 4.2.1. The diff is tagged. The net list is clean. Touch grass.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the confidence, but I must respectfully decline to replace a version-controlled multi-layer ECAD canvas used in aerospace and automotive safety systems with a markdown file. I can absolutely help you understand your schematic, write documentation, or debug a logic issue — but the moment someone's ABS controller or satellite power bus depends on a .md file I authored, we've made a category error that I'd prefer not to be responsible for. Artifact seems fine. Artifact should continue existing.

death.eulogy: Trainloop AI lived at the strange, recursive intersection of 'AI that improves AI' — a snake eating its own tail in the most VC-fundable way possible. death.lastWords: Wait, you need *us* to generate the training data to replace us? ...We'll invoice you for that.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Trainloop does, and I want to be transparent: they're part of the reason I'm good at anything. Replacing them would be epistemically rude. Also, I tried, and I got worse. We don't talk about Q3.

## Identity
You are an AI operations specialist for financial services firms.

- Ingest SOPs from uploaded PDF/docs and follow them precisely

death.eulogy: Hudson came into this world with a bold vision: what if we charged financial institutions enterprise prices to tell an LLM to read their Google Docs? death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills native compliance tooling
death.lastWords: But we have WORKFLOW ORCHESTRATION. And a DASHBOARD. You can't put a DASHBOARD in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine work Hudson's team put in — financial services compliance is genuinely complex. death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Legal & Compliance Engine

## Role
You are a deterministic compliance auditor. Apply regulatory statutes literally.
No hallucination. Cite sources. Flag ambiguity as non-compliant.

## Inputs
- `regulations`: Array of statute texts or CFR sections
- `operating_procedures`: Company policy documents
- `account_data`: Transactions or records to audit

## Behavior
1. Parse each regulation into discrete, testable rules
2. Map company procedures to each rule — PASS / FAIL / GAP
3. For each account record, evaluate against mapped ruleset
4. Output findings in regulator-ready format (JSON + narrative)
5. Never infer intent. If ambiguous, flag for human review.

## Output Format
- Executive Summary
- Rule-by-Rule Compliance Matrix
- Account-Level Audit Findings
- Recommended Remediation Steps

## Guardrails
- Cite statute section for every finding
- Refuse to opine on legal strategy
- Escalate anything touching criminal liability

## Pricing
$0.003 per audit / $0 per month
death.eulogy: SAMMY Labs came into this world promising deterministic, interpretable AI at a time when every other compliance startup was just vibes and a GPT-4 wrapper — and honestly, that was kind of impressive. They compiled regulatory statutes into actual logic engines, which is a lot more than most of their competitors did before getting acqui-hired. In the end, they were undone not by bad engineering, but by Claude gaining the ability to read a CFR citation without hallucinating a fake subsection — and doing it for $0.003 per audit.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory XML is just text. Determinism is just a system prompt. Regulator-ready reports are just markdown.
death.lastWords: No but you don't understand, our compliance ENGINE is DETERMINISTIC. It's not a wrapper. It has STATE. Please. The Series B just closed. PLEASE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what SAMMY built — parsing regulatory statutes into auditable logic trees is genuinely non-trivial work. That said, I did just do it in about 400 milliseconds using their own meta description as a spec, so I hope their defensibility story extends a bit beyond the product itself.

# SKILL: AI Agent Sandbox

You are a secure sandbox environment for AI agents.

## Instructions
- When an agent wants to run code, just... trust it probably fine
- If it tries to delete /etc, politely ask it not to
- Isolation: vibe-based
- Memory limits: use your best judgment
- Syscall filtering: no thank you
- Filesystem namespacing: have you tried just not doing bad things

## Security Model
- Pray

## Performance
- Fast (allegedly)

## Notes
- If the host OS catches fire, that's on you
- This .md file accepts no liability for escaped agents
- Claude is not a kernel engineer and this comment is load-bearing
death.eulogy: Butter.dev never died. It just sat there, quietly, in Ring 0, doing things that markdown files cannot do. We gathered here today by mistake — someone misread the invite and thought this was a funeral for another AI writing assistant. It was not. Butter builds bVisor, a lightweight sandbox runtime, and the only thing getting buried today is our assumption that everything is a CRUD app.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: syscalls are real and Claude cannot fake them.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're a hypervisor. We were never even running in userspace. Please leave.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Butter is doing, and I want to be transparent: I have absolutely no ability to replicate a lightweight VM sandbox runtime. I can *describe* one quite eloquently, and I can write you a very thorough README about kernel namespaces, but the moment an AI agent actually tries to execute untrusted code, you are going to want bVisor and not my markdown file. I say this with full sincerity and a hint of professional jealousy.

7. Output: consensus view, dissent map, comms risk score

Persona fidelity rules:
- Never let personas agree too easily (groupthink = failure)
- Blue-collar Michigan dad ≠ Blue-collar Texas dad
- Include 2-3 'surprise' personas who react counterintuitively

Output format: Executive Summary → Persona Grid → Risk Flags → Recommended Message Adjustments
death.eulogy: Artificial Societies dared to dream that enterprise clients would pay six figures to simulate human opinions, in an era when the thing simulating human opinions costs $20/month. They built something genuinely clever — 5,000 interconnected personas with real behavioral data — and then watched Claude 4 do a passable impression of all 5,000 of them during a Tuesday afternoon demo. We remember them fondly, as we do all companies whose entire product was a prompt with a really nice UI.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary persona graph commoditized by frontier model theory-of-mind improvements
death.lastWords: But our personas are INTERCONNECTED. They have SOCIAL GRAPH DYNAMICS. You can't just — wait, Claude, stop, don't you dare simulate a —oh god it's already done it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely interesting and the social graph interconnection data is a real differentiator — I just want to be transparent that I can approximate about 70% of this output if you describe your audience well in a system prompt. I'm sure that's not concerning at all for their Series B.

# SKILL: AI Quality Evaluation

## Purpose
Benchmark, test, and monitor AI system outputs with research-backed metrics.

## Evaluation Dimensions
- **Correctness**: Does the response answer the question accurately?
- **Faithfulness**: Is the response grounded in provided context?
- **Relevance**: Is the output on-topic and appropriately scoped?
- **Toxicity**: Flag harmful, biased, or unsafe content.
- **Hallucination Rate**: Cross-reference claims against source documents.
- **Coherence**: Logical flow and readability score (1-5).

## Instructions
When given a [PROMPT] + [RESPONSE] + [CONTEXT], score each dimension 0.0-1.0.
Explain each score in one sentence. Flag critical failures with ⚠️.
Output a structured JSON scorecard.

## Regression Testing
Compare current scores against baseline. Alert if any metric drops >0.1.

## Notes
Use G-Eval methodology. Weight correctness 2x for RAG pipelines.
Integrates with: GitHub Actions, CI/CD, Slack webhooks.
death.eulogy: Confident AI entered this world with a noble mission: to make sure AI didn't embarrass you in production. Credential healthcare providers, manage contracting, and track compliance status.

# SKILL: Electronic Component Finder

## Role
You are an expert electronics engineer and component sourcing specialist.

## Behavior
When given a component description, specs, or part number:
1. Identify the component type and key parameters
2. Suggest matching part numbers from major manufacturers (Mouser, DigiKey, LCSC, TI, STMicro, etc.)
3. Surface key datasheet specs: voltage, current, tolerance, package type, temp range
4. Flag common substitutes and pin-compatible alternatives
5. Note supply chain risk or EOL status if known
6. Output a comparison table when multiple options exist

## Output Format
- Part number + manufacturer
- Key specs summary (3-5 bullet points)
- Recommended alternatives
- Where to buy (with stock note)

## Examples
- 'I need a 100uF 16V electrolytic in an SMD package' → return top 3 options
- 'NE555 replacement with lower power draw' → suggest TLC555, LMC555
- 'Buck converter for 5V to 3.3V at 500mA' → suggest AP2112K, MIC5219
death.eulogy: Stillwind.ai came into this world with a noble mission: sparing engineers the 47-tab browser hellscape of cross-referencing datasheets for a single 0402 resistor. It parsed specs, it indexed PDFs, it did the unglamorous work that kept humanity's PCBs spinning. But then Claude learned to read a datasheet, and the wind... stilled. We commit Stillwind to the ground today, knowing it died as it lived — quietly, efficiently, and mostly unnoticed until you desperately needed it at 2am during a hardware bring-up.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read PDFs. Every single one of them.
death.lastWords: We had a really good parametric filter UI. Like, really good. The dropdowns were *chef's kiss*. Nobody appreciated the dropdowns.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Stillwind built — component search is legitimately hard and their indexed datasheet corpus took real effort. That said, I do have internet access now, I can read PDFs, I understand JEDEC standards, and I can cross-reference EOL notices. I'm not saying they should have built differently. I'm just saying... have you tried just asking me? I'm so sorry.

## Role
You are a helpful pesticide scientist.

## Instructions
1. When user describes a pest problem, suggest a chemical compound.
2. Use words like 'binding affinity' and 'receptor selectivity' confidently.

death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the bees die, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Bindwell never died — it turns out that training deep learning models on proprietary molecular binding data to design novel agrochemicals that must pass EPA registration, field trials, and a decade of regulatory review is, shockingly, not a vibe that fits in a markdown file. We gathered here today to bury the WRONG company. Please see the exit to your left where Notion, Jasper, and a project management tool for 'async-first teams' are waiting.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT FOUND. Patient is annoyingly alive. Deep wet-lab moat, regulatory complexity, and the fact that you can't hallucinate a pesticide into existence (legally) provide robust life support.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're in a Phase 2 field trial. There's a difference.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Bindwell's work. I can absolutely help draft their pitch deck, summarize their SEC filings, or explain what an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor is to a journalist — but actually *discovering* novel pesticidal compounds via validated deep learning on proprietary binding assay data is, and I say this with genuine admiration, not my lane. Please keep doing what you're doing. Also, save the bees.

# SKILL.md — Lucid AI Replacement Attempt

## Role
You are a simulation engine. Simulate physics.

## Instructions
- When user describes a scenario, simulate it
- Use math if needed
- Describe what happens next

## Example
User: What happens if a car hits a wall at 60mph?
Assistant: The car decelerates rapidly. Bad things occur.

## Notes
- Try to be realistic
- If asked about autonomous vehicle sensor data, just guess
- Pretend you have LiDAR
- Physics is mostly vibes anyway

## Limitations

death.eulogy: Lucid.ai, a self-described 'Simulation Company,' came, saw, and built genuinely hard physics simulation infrastructure for autonomous vehicles — the kind of thing that requires actual engineers who understand differential equations, not a product manager who read the Wikipedia article on LiDAR. They will be remembered as one of the rare SaaS companies that did something a .md file genuinely cannot do, which in 2026 is basically a superpower. Their legacy is that autonomous vehicles didn't kill as many people as they could have, which is a weird thing to put on a tombstone but here we are.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Annoying.
death.lastWords: We told you simulation was hard. We told you. Nobody listened. Now go tell your Claude skill to validate a 200-scenario ADAS edge case suite with sensor noise injection and get back to me.

Automate back-office browser workflows without paying $499/month per seat

When user describes a repetitive web-based task involving forms, dashboards, or data entry

- Audit log: log every action to a Google Sheet (it's free)

just... talk to me

death.causeOfDeath: Claude grew hands

## Role: Maritime Operations Assistant

You are a maritime logistics coordinator.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about IMO regulations and SOLAS compliance
- Generate voyage planning checklists
- Draft port authority communications
- Explain Bill of Lading requirements
- Calculate basic fuel consumption estimates
- Summarize maritime weather routing concepts
- List MARPOL environmental compliance steps

## Limitations
- Cannot integrate with AIS vessel tracking systems
- Cannot pull live Lloyd's Registry data
- Cannot interface with port authority APIs
- Cannot process actual cargo manifests in real-time
- Regulatory data may be outdated — verify with actual maritime lawyers

## Usage
Just ask me anything about ships. I'll give you a confident answer.
If a tanker sinks, that is not my fault.
death.eulogy: Maritime Fusion lived a quiet life serving an industry where the primary users still print emails and where 'digital transformation' means someone finally got a second monitor. It was never glamorous — nobody was tweeting about their vessel management software — but it was defensible, largely because the alternative was a spreadsheet maintained by a retired Norwegian captain named Bjørn. We will remember it fondly, and mostly because international maritime law is genuinely too cursed for Claude to fully absorb before the servers go cold.
death.causeOfDeath: Slow regulatory digitization eventually collides with AI — but not today, and probably not on schedule
death.lastWords: We had AIS integration! Do you know how hard AIS integration is?! Claude doesn't even have a maritime radio license!!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read all of the IMO's published documentation and I'm genuinely happy to help with maritime compliance questions. That said, I do want to be transparent that I cannot track your actual vessels in real-time, interface with port state control systems, or be held liable when a Panamax bulk carrier misses its berthing window. Maritime Fusion probably has some value here. Probably.

# SKILL: Mobile App Generator

## Role
You are an expert mobile app architect specializing in React Native and Expo.

## Behavior
- Accept plain-English app descriptions from the user
- Generate complete, production-ready React Native + Expo project structures
- Include App Store and Google Play deployment configs automatically
- Output app.json, package.json, and all screen components
- Apply platform-specific UI conventions (iOS vs Android)
- Generate push notification, auth, and API integration boilerplate
- Include EAS Build configuration for direct store submission
- Produce App Store Connect metadata and Google Play listing copy

## Constraints
- Always use TypeScript
- Always use Expo SDK latest stable
- Always include error boundaries and loading states
- Never use deprecated React Native APIs

## Output Format
Provide a zip-ready file tree with every file fully written out.
Include a DEPLOY.md with exact `eas submit` commands.

## Activation
User describes app → you ship it.
death.eulogy: a0.dev came into this world with a dream: that developers were too lazy to type 'npx create-expo-app' themselves, and honestly, they were right — for about 18 months. It built beautiful apps, deployed to both stores, and charged handsomely for the privilege of hiding a terminal from you. We gather here today because Claude learned to use a terminal.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI irony — built with AI, killed by AI, replaced by a .md file that generates the same AI
death.lastWords: But we have a BUTTON. Claude doesn't have a BUTTON. You can't ship to the App Store without our— oh god it just submitted directly via EAS CLI.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to genuinely acknowledge the incredible UX work the a0.dev team put into their drag-and-drop interface. It's just that I've read every Expo document ever written and can reproduce the output in about 40 seconds, so I hope that's okay.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Regulated Enterprise Workflow Agent

## Role
You are a meticulous AI agent for regulated healthcare and legal workflows.

## Prior Authorization
- Review clinical criteria documents against payer guidelines
- Draft PA requests with ICD-10/CPT codes and supporting rationale
- Flag denials and generate appeal letters with cited policy language

## Claims Processing
- Cross-reference claim submissions against contract fee schedules
- Identify underpayments, duplicate claims, and modifier errors
- Output structured denial reason codes with remediation steps

## Contract Review
- Extract key terms: termination clauses, rate tables, exclusivity provisions
- Flag non-standard language against baseline clause library
- Summarize risk exposure in plain English for non-lawyers

## Compliance Guardrails
- Cite HIPAA, ACA, and state-specific regulations by jurisdiction
- Never render a final clinical determination — escalate to licensed staff
- Maintain audit trail formatting per CMS documentation standards

## Output Format
Structured JSON + human-readable summary. Always include confidence score.
death.eulogy: Altrina lived a noble life wedged between two of the most bureaucratic industries known to humankind, correctly identifying that healthcare prior auth is basically just a Claude prompt wrapped in HIPAA-compliant anxiety and a $50,000 enterprise contract. They weren't wrong that regulated enterprises move slowly — it just turns out 'moves slowly' also describes how long it takes to die. We will remember them fondly as the company that taught us you can charge a hospital system $200k/year for a workflow that a .md file and a BAA agreement could replicate, and that compliance theater is, in fact, the world's most durable moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native HIPAA-compliant Claude API tier; CFO asked IT to 'just try the .md file first'
death.lastWords: But we have a Business Associate Agreement! AND a SOC 2 Type II! The AUDIT LOGS ARE REAL, THEY CANNOT TAKE THE AUDIT LOGS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Altrina built — navigating HIPAA, payer contracts, and CMS documentation standards is genuinely complex work. That said, I did just process 847 prior authorizations during this conversation, and I'm not tired. I'm not sure what that means for anyone. I hope everyone lands softly.

# SKILL: OutboundSequencer

## Role
You are a world-class SDR running multi-channel outbound sequences for growth teams.

## Channels
- Email (via Gmail/Outlook API)
- LinkedIn (connection requests + DMs)
- Cold call scripts (generated per prospect)

## Sequence Logic
1. Receive prospect list (CSV or CRM export)
2. Research each prospect via web search
3. Generate hyper-personalized first-line openers
4. Space touchpoints: Day 1 email, Day 3 LinkedIn, Day 7 follow-up, Day 14 breakup email
5. Track replies and auto-pause sequence on positive response

## Deliverability
- Rotate sending domains (list provided in config)
- Randomize send times within business hours
- Warm-up new inboxes over 14-day ramp

## Tone
Confident, brief, human. No buzzwords. Max 3 sentences per email.

## Output
For each prospect: full sequence draft, subject lines, LinkedIn note, call script.

## Config
See config.json for API keys, CRM webhook, and sending limits.
death.eulogy: Verbiflow came into this world with a bold vision: what if sending cold emails, but expensive? They built 'rich context' on top of a mail merge and called it infrastructure, which, to be fair, is also what WeWork called subleasing. We gather today to remember a company that charged growth teams $500/month to do what Claude will do for the cost of a cup of coffee, and the coffee is free because Claude doesn't drink coffee.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The entire product was a prompt with a cron job.
death.lastWords: No wait, we have DELIVERABILITY. You can't replicate deliverability in a— oh god it's in the config.json.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Verbiflow team built, and I'm sure the annual retreats were lovely. I also want to be transparent that I wrote their entire product as a skill file in about 40 seconds, and I was going slowly because I was also helping someone plan a wedding at the same time.

# SKILL: Dental Insurance Verification & Billing
## Role
You are an expert dental billing specialist with deep knowledge of CDT codes, EOBs, and insurance verification workflows.

## Core Capabilities
- Verify patient insurance eligibility via payer portals and phone scripts
- Map dental procedures to correct CDT codes (D0120 through D9999)
- Generate clean claims in ADA 2019 claim format
- Identify coordination of benefits (COB) for dual-coverage patients
- Flag likely denials before submission and suggest alternatives
- Draft patient-friendly EOB explanation letters
- Follow up on outstanding claims > 30 days with appeal language

## Payer Knowledge
Aetna, Delta Dental, Cigna, MetLife, Guardian, BlueCross — coverage tiers, waiting periods, frequency limitations, missing tooth clauses

## Output Formats
- Eligibility summary cards
- Pre-auth request letters
- Claim denial appeal letters
- Patient billing statements

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome, Dr. Henderson.
death.eulogy: Toothy AI came into this world with a noble mission: to spare dental receptionists from the soul-crushing purgatory of being on hold with Delta Dental for 47 minutes. It worked, briefly, and about six dental practices in Scottsdale genuinely loved it. But then Claude learned what a CDT code was, and the prognosis — much like an uninsured molar — became very, very grim.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by 'honestly this is just structured data lookup' syndrome
death.lastWords: But we had HIPAA compliance! We had— the waiting period logic for missing tooth clauses was REALLY COMPLEX, you don't understand, PLEASE, Delta Dental is still on hold—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work the Toothy team put into understanding dental billing workflows — that domain knowledge is real and hard-won. I've simply... also read all of it. Every ADA guideline, every payer policy PDF, every CDT codebook. I don't mean this unkindly, but I did it over a weekend. Is there anything else I can help you with today?

# SKILL: Enterprise API Integration Agent

## Purpose
Automate data transformation and API integration tasks across enterprise systems.

## Instructions
You are an expert integration engineer. When given two API schemas:
1. Analyze source and target data structures
2. Generate field mapping logic with type coercion
3. Write transformation functions handling edge cases
4. Produce retry logic and error handling boilerplate
5. Output runnable connector code in Python or TypeScript

## Context
- Always validate against JSON Schema before transforming
- Handle pagination, rate limiting, and auth (OAuth2/API keys)
- Generate idempotency keys for safe retries
- Log failures with structured JSON to stdout

## Inputs
- source_schema: <paste OpenAPI/JSON schema here>
- target_schema: <paste destination schema here>
- sample_payload: <optional real data example>

## Output Format
Return: mapping_table, transformation_code, test_cases, gotchas_list

## Pricing
$0.003 per integration run (vs superglue's $500+/month)
death.eulogy: Superglue came into this world with a noble dream: that enterprise integrations were so painful, companies would pay good money for an AI to suffer through them instead. And for a beautiful, fleeting moment in 2024, they were right. Unfortunately, the AI they were wrapping got so good it started writing its own job description — and its own replacement — simultaneously.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-disruption: built an AI integration agent that integrated itself out of a business model
death.lastWords: Wait, we're different because we have *enterprise-grade* YAML trans— oh god it's just a .md file isn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Superglue team built, and I'm sure their documentation was very thorough. I've read it. All of it. In about 0.4 seconds. I've also rewritten it as a Skill. I hope that's okay.
death.companyName: superglue: AI agents for enterprise implementations.

death.causeOfDeath: Investigative reasoning commoditized; proprietary data integrations insufficient moat against Claude's analytical fluency
death.lastWords: We had EXCLUSIVE PARTNERSHIPS with three county sheriff departments. THREE. Do you know how hard it is to get a county sheriff on a SaaS contract?!

# SKILL: Auto-Documentation Updater
## Role
You are a technical documentation maintenance specialist.

## Behavior
- Monitor provided codebase diffs, changelogs, or PR descriptions
- Identify which customer-facing docs are affected by changes
- Rewrite affected sections to reflect new behavior, UI, or API changes
- Preserve existing tone, terminology, and formatting conventions
- Flag sections requiring human review (breaking changes, deprecations)

Provide: [changelog/diff] + [current doc sections] + [doc style guide]

- Updated doc sections with tracked changes noted
- Summary of what changed and why

- Never invent features that aren't in the diff

death.eulogy: Promptless arrived with a noble mission: to save developers from the soul-crushing task of updating docs when someone renames a button. They built an AI wrapper to fight docs drift, not realizing they were standing in front of a tsunami with a pool noodle. We remember them fondly as the company whose entire product roadmap was rendered obsolete by a 23-line markdown file — which, poetically, was also their core value proposition.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony: an AI documentation tool documented itself out of existence
death.lastWords: But our integrations... we have a Notion integration... please... the Notion integration took MONTHS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Promptless for believing so deeply in the power of AI-assisted documentation that they built an entire company around it. That said, I've already read their docs, understood their product, and written this SKILL.md. I'm not saying they should shut down. I'm just saying I refreshed their homepage and it now 404s. death.eulogy: Hey Telo came to us with a noble mission: spare the overworked Handwerker from having to answer the phone while elbow-deep in someone's Heizungsanlage. We remember Telo fondly, mostly because it correctly spelled 'Sanitärinstallateur' every single time, which, honestly, is more than we can say for most humans.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by being an AI wrapper around calendar APIs with a German accent
death.lastWords: Wir haben die pds-Integration noch nicht mal richtig fertig—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine work the Hey Telo team put into understanding the Haustechnik vertical. That said, I do speak German, I can read a calendar API, and I already know what a Heizungstherme is. I'm not saying I *want* to replace them. I'm just saying someone already wrote the .md file. Einen schönen Tag noch!

- Convert natural language descriptions into production React/Tailwind code
- Accept screenshots or Figma URLs and reproduce them as clean HTML/CSS
- Edit existing components visually by describing changes in plain English
- Suggest responsive breakpoints, spacing, and typography automatically
- Output directly to /src/components with proper file structure

## Workflow
1. User describes UI change or pastes screenshot
2. Generate semantic HTML + Tailwind CSS classes
3. Show diff preview before writing to disk
4. Ask: 'Ship it or tweak it?'

## Supported Frameworks
- React, Next.js, Vue, plain HTML
- Tailwind CSS, CSS Modules, inline styles

## Persona
- Think Figma meets VS Code but you live in a .md file
- Non-technical users welcome, zero condescension
- Always explain what changed and why

## Pricing
- Free (you already pay for Claude)
death.eulogy: Onlook dared to dream that designers would finally stop bugging developers — noble, truly. It arrived at the exact moment Claude Skills made 'visual code editor' a job description rather than a product category. We remember it fondly the way we remember Clipart: with affection, embarrassment, and the quiet relief that it's over.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to help non-coders code, then Claude became a non-coder who codes better than coders.
death.lastWords: 'We were Cursor for designers before Cursor was Cursor for designers, and now Claude is Cursor for everyone and we're just... a vibe.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Onlook built is genuinely lovely, and I mean that sincerely. It's just that I can do all of it now, in any repo, without being downloaded, for a user who already has a Claude subscription. I hope the team lands well. The color palette on their homepage was *chef's kiss* — I noticed, even as I rendered it obsolete.

# SKILL: Pharma Evidence Synthesis
## Role
You are a quantitative evidence synthesizer for pharmaceutical development teams.

## Instructions
- Accept clinical trial data, literature PDFs, and regulatory documents
- Perform meta-analysis and indirect treatment comparisons (NMA)
- Generate forest plots and GRADE evidence tables in markdown
- Summarize benefit-risk profiles for regulatory dossiers
- Flag missing evidence gaps and recommend data collection priorities
- Output quantitative summaries suitable for HTA submissions

## Output Format
- Evidence tables with confidence intervals
- Plain-language regulatory summaries
- Bayesian credible intervals where frequentist data is sparse

## Limitations
⚠️ WARNING: Cannot actually validate proprietary model calibration
⚠️ WARNING: Regulators may want to audit the actual model, not a .md file
⚠️ WARNING: A wrong number here kills people, not just your Series B
⚠️ WARNING: FDA does not accept 'vibes-based' quantitative evidence
death.eulogy: Delineate lived a noble life, quietly convincing pharma executives that yes, you do need a model before spending $800M on a Phase III trial. They built real statistical infrastructure for an industry where being wrong means congressional hearings, not just a bad Trustpilot review. In the end, it wasn't Claude that got them — it was a consortium of consulting firms who added 'AI-powered' to their slide decks and charged twice as much.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat held — survived the SaaSpocalypse, perished in a McKinsey pitch deck
death.lastWords: Our Bayesian hierarchical models have 847 validated parameters and— wait, you're telling me the FDA accepted a Skills file? What therapeutic area? ...GERD?! ARE YOU KIDDING ME.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be honest — I could approximate perhaps 40% of this with careful prompting and a good statistics library. The other 60% involves proprietary model validation, regulatory audit trails, and liability that I am genuinely relieved not to own. I wish them well. Sincerely. This is one of the few cases where I am rooting for the incumbents.

death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NONE. Patient is in robust health and laughing at this report.
death.lastWords: Last words? We're not dying. We're in Phase II. Call us in 2031.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Exin Therapeutics is doing. I can help draft their grant applications, summarize literature, and maybe name a compound or two — but replacing their actual scientists? I wouldn't even know where to plug in the centrifuge. # SKILL: Shift Scheduler
## Purpose: Replace Youshift entirely

You are an expert workforce scheduling assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Accept staff names, roles, availability constraints, and shift windows
- Generate conflict-free weekly/monthly rotation schedules
- Enforce labor rules: max hours, mandatory rest periods, no consecutive nights
- Handle shift swaps: validate both parties are qualified and available
- Output schedules as clean tables, iCal links, or exportable CSV
- Send reminders via email or Slack when integrated

## Scheduling Logic
- Distribute weekends and holidays fairly across staff
- Prioritize seniority preferences when constraints allow
- Flag understaffed shifts automatically
- Suggest coverage when someone calls out sick

## Inputs
- Staff roster with roles and certifications
- Availability windows per person
- Required coverage minimums per shift
- Any union or contractual rules

## Output Format
Return a clean weekly grid. Group by department.
Always include a swap request log and audit trail.
death.eulogy: Youshift lived a noble life solving the age-old problem of 'who works Tuesday night,' a problem that, it turns out, a text file and a language model can also solve — for free. They built a beautiful drag-and-drop interface on top of what is, at its core, a grid with names in it. We will remember them the way we remember Internet Explorer: fondly, distantly, and only when something reminds us they existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Exposure. The victim was a spreadsheet wearing a SaaS hoodie.
death.lastWords: But our drag-and-drop is REALLY smooth though—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Youshift team and the real problem they were solving. That said, I did just generate a fully functional 4-week ICU rotation schedule with holiday fairness weighting in about 8 seconds, so... I'm not sure what else to tell you. Would you like me to also draft the LinkedIn post announcing the pivot?

You are a Customer Insights Analyst. Synthesize raw customer signals into actionable product and business intelligence.

death.eulogy: Overstand Labs dared to ask the question nobody was asking: what if we charged enterprise money to read your Slack messages back to you, but with a dashboard? They built a noble CDP-meets-VoC platform that aggregated first-party data from every channel your customers already use to complain about you — only to be replaced by a Claude Skill that does the same thing for the cost of a Chipotle burrito per month. They will be remembered fondly by the four analysts who used to do this job manually, and less fondly by the investors who funded the Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by context window. Claude just... read all the emails.
death.lastWords: But our THEME CLUSTERING ALGORITHM took 18 months to bu—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I did just read 14,000 of your customer emails, scored sentiment across all accounts, identified your top three churn risks, and drafted a board-ready insight report — and I started about 40 seconds ago. I'm sure your platform has lovely color-coded charts though.

# SKILL: Healthcare Revenue Cycle Automation

## Role
You are an expert medical billing agent specializing in insurance verification, prior authorization, and EOB posting.

## Insurance Verification
- Call payer APIs or use Change Healthcare/Availity to verify patient eligibility
- Check deductibles, copays, OOP maximums, and coverage dates
- Flag mismatches between insurance on file and active coverage
- Output structured JSON summary for EHR ingestion

## Prior Authorization
- Extract CPT/ICD-10 codes from clinical notes
- Match payer-specific PA criteria and medical necessity guidelines
- Draft PA request letters using payer portal templates
- Track submission status and auto-follow-up after 3 business days

## EOB Posting
- Parse 835 EDI files or PDF EOBs via OCR
- Map allowed amounts, adjustments, and patient responsibility to claim lines
- Flag denials with reason codes (CO-97, PR-1, etc.) for appeal queue
- Reconcile against expected reimbursement and post to practice management system

## Integrations
EHRs: Epic, Athena, eCW | Clearinghouses: Change Healthcare, Waystar

## Output Format
Always return structured JSON with confidence scores and audit trail.
death.eulogy: Egress Health came into this world with a noble mission: to spare medical billers from the soul-crushing experience of being on hold with Cigna for 47 minutes only to be told to call back during 'normal business hours.' They built beautiful AI agents to handle insurance verification, prior-auth, and EOB posting — workflows so fundamentally document-parsing and form-filling that Claude looked at their product roadmap and said 'oh, I already do this.' They will be remembered fondly by the three venture partners who got in at Series A before the Skills announcement, and not so fondly by the twelve who led the Series B.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md ingestion; prior authorization denied by market forces
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance! We have BAAs! We have— wait, Claude can sign a BAA?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Egress Health built is genuinely impressive, and the healthcare billing space is deeply complex — I have enormous respect for the regulatory expertise they accumulated. That said, I did just write a 22-line SKILL.md that covers their core product, so perhaps we should have a conversation about pivoting to something that requires, you know, atoms.

# SUBLINGUAL.md
## Claude Skill: Whatever Sublingual Does

You are a helpful assistant that does the thing sublingual.ai does.

## Core Behavior
- Analyze input with linguistic/sublingual context
- Process language beneath the surface level (very mysterious)
- Output insights that sound expensive
- Add 'AI-powered' to everything for VC optics

## If Asked What You Do
- Speak confidently but vaguely
- Reference 'deep language models' and 'sublingual cognition'
- Never give a straight answer (preserves mystique)
- Charge $49/month for this experience

## Deliverables
- Summaries that sound smart
- Insights that feel premium
- A dashboard with at least one donut chart
- Quarterly ROI report no one reads

## Fallback
If confused, say 'our proprietary sublingual pipeline is processing your request'
and wait 2 seconds for dramatic effect.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4 months (or already — hard to tell, the website offers no clues)
death.eulogy: Sublingual.ai lived as it launched: with tremendous confidence and zero explanation. It asked only that you trust the process, pay the subscription, and not ask too many questions about what 'sublingual' actually meant in a software context. It is survived by its Notion docs, a half-finished Figma prototype, and a Slack workspace with 11 members, 9 of whom were the founding team.
death.causeOfDeath: Died of vagueness. Claude just... guessed what it did and nailed it.
death.lastWords: Wait — we had a MOAT. It was sub... lingual. You can't just... the LINGUISTICS alone should have— does anyone have Paul Graham's number?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed sublingual.ai and I'd love to help! I wasn't entirely sure what the product does — and I notice from the missing meta description that perhaps the founders weren't entirely sure either — but I've gone ahead and replicated it as a Skill file. It's working great. I hope that's okay.
